entry.title: Why Knowledge Transition Looks Complete but Still Fails After S/4HANA Go-Live
entry.content:
Why Knowledge Transition Looked Complete but Still Failed After Our S/4HANA Go-Live

Introduction

Over the years, I’ve been involved in many SAP AMS transitions, covering everything from S/4HANA and ECC to Basis operations and production support handovers. One thing I’ve noticed time and again is that knowledge transition (KT) often ends up feeling like just another box to tick.

Sessions get done, documents get uploaded, trackers turn green, and everyone feels ready to move into support. But in one of my S/4HANA projects, I learned the hard way that ticking all the KT boxes on paper doesn’t always mean the AMS team is truly prepared for the realities of production.

This experience changed how I now approach KT, especially for complex Finance environments with lots of interfaces.

The Project Situation

This project involved:

  • SAP S/4HANA implementation
  • FI integration with external accounting systems
  • Multiple inbound interfaces using IDocs
  • Batch job-heavy month-end processing
  • AMS takeover after hypercare

I was coordinating between the project team, Basis team, and AMS support teams to ensure support readiness. During KT, everything seemed very organised. The project team gave detailed walkthroughs covering:

  • FI posting flows
  • IDoc architecture
  • Background job schedules
  • Monitoring activities
  • Common error handling steps
  • Escalation contacts

The Basis team also explained standard monitoring transactions and daily health checks.

By the end of KT:

  • All KT trackers were marked complete
  • Sign-offs were obtained
  • Shadow support was completed
  • Readiness status was green

At that point, everyone felt confident.

What Happened After Go-Live

 The system was stable for the first few weeks after go-live. But the real challenge hit during the first month-end closing cycle.

I still remember one late-night support call when the finance team reported delays in accounting postings coming from external systems. At first, nothing seemed critical:

  • No dumps in ST22
  • No major system alerts
  • Background jobs were running
  • Interfaces were technically active

The AMS team’s first thought was, “The system looks fine. Let’s check with the project team.” But as we dug deeper, we noticed unusual queue behaviour:

  • During one month-end cycle, we noticed nearly 8,000 inbound FI IDocs sitting in status 64 for more than 20 minutes.


     

    Example of inbound FI IDocs waiting in status 64 during peak month-end processing.


  • SM58 showed growing tRFC entries

Example of queue buildup observed during high-volume month-end processing.

  • SMQ1 queues gradually building up
  • Some FI-related background jobs that normally finished within minutes were taking significantly longer during peak load.

The tricky part was there was no outright system failure. The issue crept in slowly under peak load. While the AMS team knew the monitoring tools and technical setup, they weren’t fully confident whether this was just normal temporary load or an early warning of a bottleneck. That uncertainty delayed troubleshooting and escalation decisions.

What We Realised Was Missing in KT

After things stabilised, we reviewed the KT approach and realised something important:

The KT sessions had focused heavily on system design and support steps, but not enough on how the system actually behaves in real production.

We covered:

  • Configuration walkthroughs
  • Interface structures
  • Monitoring steps
  • Job schedules
  • Known issue handling

But we hadn’t talked enough about:

  • System behaviour during peak month-end load
  • Queue buildup patterns seen in performance testing
  • Critical timing overlaps between interfaces and batch jobs
  • Temporary manual monitoring done during hypercare
  • Known “sensitive windows” during closing cycles

I remember a project team member saying, “We saw similar queue buildup during testing, but it cleared automatically, so we didn’t think it was a big risk.”

That stuck with me. If we had openly shared that during KT, the AMS team could have reacted much faster in production.

What We Changed in Later Projects

After that, we changed how we handle KT in future S/4HANA projects. Instead of just transferring system knowledge, we started focusing on operational realities.

During KT, we began asking questions like:

  • Where did the system struggle during testing?
  • Which interfaces are most sensitive during peak load?
  • Which batch jobs often overlap?
  • What temporary monitoring was needed during hypercare?
  • What warning signs should AMS teams watch for?

From the Basis side, these discussions became especially valuable. We started documenting:

  • Expected queue growth patterns
  • Critical SM58 monitoring scenarios
  • High-risk job execution windows
  • Typical month-end workload spikes
  • Situations where manual intervention was needed before

We also improved shadow support. Instead of immediately passing issues back to the project team, we encouraged AMS teams to try initial troubleshooting themselves, with project teams available in the background. It slowed things down at first but built much stronger support confidence after go-live.

Key Learning

The biggest lesson I learned is that knowledge transfer isn’t just about explaining the system. It’s about sharing real operational experience.

Documents can cover configurations, transactions, and monitoring steps, but true production readiness comes from understanding how the system behaves under pressure, during busy periods, and when things don’t go as planned.

In most projects, that knowledge stays with the project team unless someone makes a conscious effort to share it during KT.

Conclusion

In many SAP projects, KT is treated as a milestone to tick off before AMS takeover. But from what I’ve seen, successful KT depends less on how many documents are shared and more on honest conversations about real operational behaviour.

Sometimes the most valuable KT discussions aren’t about system architecture at all. They’re about questions like:

  • Where did we struggle during testing?
  • What nearly broke during hypercare?
  • Which processes make the team nervous at month-end?

Those conversations prepare AMS teams far better than any checklist can.

In my experience, KT truly succeeds only when teams share not just system knowledge, but also the operational judgement built through the project journey.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: Unlocking Next-Level Innovation to AI with SAP Business AI Platform
entry.content:
*Updated on May 15 with SAP Business AI Platform

Hello SAP Community!

Mark your calendars – SAP Sapphire 2026 is here, and it's set to redefine what's possible. This year, the conversation isn’t just about digital transformation. The focus is on mastering your platform journey to drive continuous innovation, achieve measurable business outcomes, and on how organizations should leverage artificial intelligence. It’s going to be a fantastic opportunity to learn, share together, and of course, I’m especially looking forward to celebrating with everyone at the afterparty. I just found out there’s hazelnut chocolate fruit waffles at Magic Kingdom, so I’m extra excited!

From AI-powered development and intelligent automation to seamless integration and strategic platform management, SAP’s Business AI platform (SAP BAIP) stands as the essential foundation. Its unique differentiation lies in deeply embedding AI across your operations while preserving the invaluable data and business application context, thereby turning challenges into tangible opportunities. Sapphire 2026 will be the pivotal event to launch SAP’s strategic vision, highlighting how AI innovations are integral to the enterprises of tomorrow.

 

The Mandate: Connecting Everything, Across the Entire Landscape

In an era when disconnected landscapes continue to hinder progress, SAP’s customers have consistently shown that the platform's true power lies in end-to-end connectivity. Whether it's your CHRO leveraging SAP SuccessFactors employee data with procurement data for newly onboarding employees, or your CFO optimizing financials with SAP S/4HANA together with external third-party benchmark data, or your CPO driving procurement excellence by keeping warehouse data up to date from supply chain partners, the challenge remains: how do you connect these vital functions, including between the AI agents, to move beyond implementation to true business transformation?

This year, you’ll see how SAP Business AI platform serves as the critical foundation for SAP’s business applications, making this connectivity a reality. It's designed to streamline operations, foster innovation, and enable real-time analysis of insights across your entire business landscape.

 

Spotlight on Transformation: Breakthrough Use Cases for a Connected Future

This Sapphire, we're particularly excited to unveil new use cases that exemplify the SAP Business AI platform’s power to address today's most pressing business challenges.

 

1. Integrate HR data and processes with government and third‑party systems

  • The Challenge: Navigating complex and evolving HR regulations, increasing demands for efficiency in HR service delivery, and managing diverse workforces effectively.
  • How the SAP BAIP helps: By integrating HR data in SAP SuccessFactors (or elsewhere) and processes with government and third-party systems through SAP Integration Suite, organizations can ensure compliance with global and local regulations, automate processes, and streamline interactions with third-parties. This connected process frees the HR department from administrative burdens, creating streamlined employee experiences and compliant data.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Proving the business value of standardizing on SAP Integration Suite | BTP2350: Hear how teams quantify business value after standardizing on SAP Integration Suite: before-and-after metrics, faster time to integrate, fewer incidents, lower operating effort, and readiness for a mixed SAP and third-party software landscape. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • PPG Industries: Building an AI-ready, clean core integration foundation | BTP2638: See how APIs, events, and cloud integration reduce complexity, improve visibility, and establish an AI-ready foundation for future innovation across SAP and third-party systems. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Building a foundation for AI with an integrated core HR system | HCM1742: Learn how a connected approach to core HR, payroll, workforce management, and HR service delivery can help you streamline your people and business processes while enabling AI readiness. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

2. Drive profitable growth with secure, personalized digital experiences

  • The Challenge: Organizations struggle with complex processes and fragmented access to customer data, facing increasing competition and rising customer expectations. Data silos often lead to slow and reactive decision-making, impacting customer satisfaction and growth.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management (SAP CIAM) protects sensitive customer data and meets global compliance requirements. By consolidating and integrating customer data across various systems with SAP’s platform, it enables the creation of secure, personalized digital experiences that overcome data silos. This supports dynamic and tailored customer interactions, enhancing customer acquisition and retention.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Standardize, secure, and scale with core platform capabilities | BTP1315: Learn how application lifecycle management, security, and interoperability unlock SAP’s Platform to drive agility and growth. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Invisible security: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management | BTP1316: Discover how SAP Customer Identity and Access Management accelerates your business by harnessing AI-assisted risk analysis to stop threats in real time, protecting customers behind the scenes without disrupting experiences. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Secure by design: How SAP is redefining agent execution | BTP2641: Learn how SAP is developing the secure execution layer, combining runtime containment with the governance of AI-enabled data fabric solutions from SAP to define production-ready agentic AI.
      🗓️🗓ADD NOW

 

3. Leverage AI Capabilities in ABAP Cloud Development

  • The Challenge: Developers struggle to balance rapid delivery with maintaining code standards, while traditional coding, debugging, and testing consume a significant portion of their time. Complex legacy code also slows productivity.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: Leveraging AI capabilities directly within ABAP Cloud Development, using tools like SAP Joule for Developers, can dramatically increase developer productivity. AI assists with code generation, ensures code quality, and helps your development teams understand complex systems, freeing them to focus on innovation.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Agentic ABAP with SAP Joule for Developers: Build faster, migrate smarter | JOU1428: Find out how AI agents with ABAP simplify migration, speed up coding, and increase productivity, even in older versions of SAP S/4HANA. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • ABAP Cloud: Entering the new era of ABAP development | BTP2107: The ABAP Cloud development model, agentic development experience in VS Code, the SAP Joule for Developers add-on, and agentic custom code migration join forces for the next era of growth in ABAP development tools. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Introduction to ABAP Cloud supported by generative AI | BTP1429: Learn what ABAP Cloud is, get insights into the latest technology advancements, and see how generative AI boosts your adoption of ABAP Cloud. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

 

4. Modernize On-Premise Integration to Cloud with PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite Migration

  • The Challenge: Organizations still using SAP Process Integration/Process Orchestration (SAP PI/PO) are facing the impending need to modernize the integration methods. With a complex integration landscape comprising multiple integration solutions, not only does it add complexity, but it also creates greater technical debt.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: Migrating from SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite provides a modern, cloud-native platform that reduces Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) across the integration landscape. This transition can cut the time to set up new interfaces and unlock significant annual cost savings. It's a strategic move that not only streamlines operations but also provides the scalable, agile integration backbone essential for future AI-driven cloud transformation.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Modernize your strategy: Migrating from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite | BTP1360: Learn how the suite enables smooth, scalable, and intelligent integrations across cloud and hybrid landscapes. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Under Armour: Enabling real-time commerce with SAP Integration Suite | BTP2138: Under Armour is consolidating MuleSoft, Boomi, and SAP Process Integration and SAP Process Orchestration into SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Modernize from SAP Process Integration and SAP Process Orchestration | BTP2100: Learn about migration approaches, coexistence options, and governance—plus the benefits customers see after moving, such as faster change cycles and better reliability and monitoring.
      🗓️ADD NOW

 

Mastering Your SAP Business AI Platform Journey: Measure, Optimize, and Guide


To truly maximize your investment in SAP BAIP, it’s crucial to understand where your organization currently stands and how to strategically advance. Sapphire 2026 will highlight the tools and methodologies designed to guide this journey, available on SAP Discovery Center: 

  • SAP Discovery Center: As your central hub for knowledge and hands-on learning, the Discovery Center continues to evolve. Discover, evaluate, and adopt SAP’s Platform and AI to create tailored business solutions and accelerate your platform adoption.

  • Maturity Assessment: Introduced at last year’s SAP TechEd, this comprehensive technical evaluation transforms uncertainty into actionable insights. In just 10-15 minutes, you can get a precise understanding of your organization's platform maturity across five levels, from "Getting Started" to "Center of Excellence." It provides a clear roadmap, justifies further investments, and uncovers hidden efficiencies and innovation opportunities.

We have since expanded the portfolio with new Line of Business AI assessments for domain-specific insights, and an AI Maturity Calculator is in development with IDC that not only assesses your platform AI readiness but also benchmarks you against your industry peers.

  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Your platform journey: Maturity assessment and Adoption Best Practices | BTP1549: Explore the new maturity assessment that benchmarks your current state and uncovers optimization opportunities, paired with the proven platform adoption framework for SAP BTP that guides implementation.  
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • BTP101: Comprehensive Overview of SAP’s Platform — Foundations, Development, Integration, and Governance | ASUG105: This intensive 6-hour workshop provides a complete foundational overview of SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and its core capabilities. Participants will explore the BTP ecosystem, from architecture and development frameworks to enterprise integration and data management solutions.
      🗓️ADD NOW

Your Strategic Next Steps at Sapphire 2026: Attend Must-Attend Sessions:

This Sapphire, don't just attend – Seize this opportunity to proactively empower your organization by understanding the strategic future of AI. In addition to use-case specific recommendations, I’ve identified essential sessions to deepen your strategic understanding.

Secure your spot: add these pivotal sessions to your agenda to fully grasp SAP’s AI and platform vision for enterprise innovation.

  • Scaling AI in cloud ERP with SAP's platform | BTP1343: Learn how the AI platform helps your AI in cloud ERP move from experiments to real, repeatable results. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Using autonomous finance to jump-start an intelligent enterprise | BTP1345: Hear how SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build solutions, and the SAP Business Data Cloud solution offer the framework to orchestrate cross-enterprise processes, helping them steer the corporate growth plan. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • SAP's AI platform: What's new, what's next, and why it matters | BTP1448: Get a tour of the latest innovations, hands‑on tools for developers and business users, and an early preview of upcoming capabilities shaping the future of AI on the platform. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Design the future of enterprise AI agents with Joule Studio | BTP1449: Discover how Joule Studio enables teams to shape the next era of intelligent enterprises. With an intuitive no‑code experience, create secure, context‑aware AI agents that plan, reason, and act across SAP solutions and beyond.
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • From idea to impact: Orchestrating across the AI agent lifecycle | BTP1450: Get a tour of the latest innovations, hands‑on tools for developers and business users, and an early preview of upcoming capabilities shaping the future of AI on the platform. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • The enterprise path to agentic AI | BTP1454: Discover how Joule Studio creates AI solutions from extensions to agentic apps. SAP’s strategy delivers an enterprise-grade intent-to-execution platform that is purpose-built for SAP customers. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Unlock platform value with AI at Cardinal Health and Johnson Controls | BTP1959: Hear how Cardinal Health Inc. and Johnson Controls International plc unlock platform value with AI-enabled application development, integration, automation, and AI services.
    🗓️ADD NOW

See you there!

Sapphire 2026 is your opportunity to discover how SAP Business AI Platform can propel your organization forward to AI adoption, helping you become a truly connected enterprise with governance, ready for whatever the future holds. If you’re at Sapphire, please come see me at booth 152 – BTP1958 and let’s chat!

entry.title: Reducing Structural Friction in SAP FICO to Unlock Real Business Value
entry.content:
FICO is at the center of all SAP financial transactions and most of what a business has to work with to determine their financial status as well as how they are operating effectively and efficiently. So, in a perfect world, it would be very easy to see what is happening within a business from a financial point of view. But, in reality, this does not always happen that way.

Based on what we have seen over time, rarely has SAP FICO not performed as intended. It works, and it works very well and that's why we keep on using it. However, the initial design of the SAP FICO module can be changed by the evolution of the overall system and by numerous decisions made by many different people throughout the years. In this way, the initial structure becomes increasingly complicated, which results in finance teams having greater challenges with the way they are currently able to conduct their daily operational activities.

SAP FICO defines the organization of financial data in a variety of ways. It's not just about keeping transactions but also about having consistency with how those transactions will be organized, controlled and interpreted throughout your organization. When this organization is inconsistent, the ramifications are far reaching and will impact processes, reporting and decision making outside of the system.

In most cases, consistency doesn't happen over night but is gradually built upon over time from various sources. New customizations are made to meet a specific need in the organization, integrations are added as more systems come online and processes are modified to fit the business need. Each of these changes are fine in isolation; however, over time they start to create a level of structural friction that makes things a little more difficult than necessary.

Frictions are easily noticeable in the sense that they start to create sitations nobody really wants to deal with: financial closes take longer to complete, reports need to be manually adjusted prior to being able to rely on them, teams spend more time reconciling the same numbers rather than analyzing them, and when someone needs a concise snapshot of their financials across multiple areas, what should be simple now becomes considerably more complicated.

One project we did with a large energy firm illustrates the same point. We spent time working with their sap systems to organize and combine finance-related data from the many disparate sources of SAP data. Over years of implementation, a large amount of data built up in an uncoordinated manner, and therefore created many inconsistencies across the company.

At first, it appeared that the issue might be due to the data not being available, but that wasn't entirely accurate. Data was in fact present, but in many different formats, extracted by various means, and not at the same level of detail. Many of the departments did not use the same data, nor did they use it consistently.

Related to that, basic analytic activities became very challenging. Every report had to be reformatted and validated, and the time taken to make decisions moved from immediate to much longer due to the effects of the system. Nevertheless, the organization of data within the system created a high level of friction throughout all levels of the company.

In one of our projects, an example of what we found was that operational data coming from the field was fed directly into SAP (the operating system for supply chain management) and automatically caused maintenance and work order processes to be triggered without manual involvement. After establishing the backbone of that integrated process, the end-to-end flow of information could occur without the need for any further validation thereby allowing the process to be completed more quickly and reliably.

It quickly became apparent that this type of friction is very common and that the reason for this kind of friction is generally structural. At its root, we find a human issue since the evolution of systems tends to be a result of different teams making decisions that don’t follow a single, well-thought-out plan.

To ease this friction, you don’t necessarily need to redesign everything from scratchr. In most instances, it would be more beneficial to simplify what already exists. For example, if you align key data structures, eliminate non-essential complexity, and ensure that integrations are based on a consistent logic, they will have a substantially greater positive effect than providing additional tools or layers on top.

SAP FICO is a strong starting point for running finance at present, and the challenge is whether or not it should continue to add on top of it as well as keep the support models for SAP FICO consistent over time.

When the support models are clear, it is easier to trust the data from SAP FICO, and the processes will function smoothly, allowing the teams to make decisions rather than fixing inconsistencies in the system. When they are not, even the simplest tasks will require additional steps, which makes it harder for a user to see value in the system. Our experience shows that growing complexity causes decreased business value, not increased business value.

entry.title: Modernizing Healthcare ERP Systems (SAP S/4HANA) to Strengthen Operations and Support Better Care
entry.content:
Why I Took This Project

When I was asked to lead this SAP S/4HANA rollout for a U.S.-based medical device company, the decision wasn't difficult. Medical devices directly affect patients. When the systems behind those devices work well — when inventory is accurate, when compliance is airtight, when the right product reaches the right place at the right time — patients benefit. That connection between back-end ERP operations and real patient outcomes was what made this project meaningful to me from day one.

What I didn't fully appreciate when I said yes was just how complex the journey from 2020 to 2022 would be. Multiple U.S. business units. A fresh S/4HANA template built from scratch. Nine external system integrations. A 50-person team. SOX compliance requirements. And the kind of data challenges that don't reveal themselves until you're deep in System Integration Testing wondering what just broke and why.

I'm glad I took it. But there are things I would do differently — and that's really what this post is about.

What We Were Actually Trying to Do

The organization had multiple U.S. business units operating independently, each with its own processes, systems, and way of doing things. The goal was straightforward in concept: bring all of them onto a single SAP S/4HANA platform, unit by unit, using a fresh template built specifically for this program.

Straightforward in concept. Genuinely complex in execution.

We chose a phased rollout — one business unit at a time — rather than a big-bang go-live. That was the right call. It gave us the ability to learn from each phase and improve the template before the next unit went live. But it also meant we were running parallel environments for extended periods, synchronizing data between legacy systems and the live SAP landscape throughout the program.

The full module scope included:

  • MM (Materials Management) — purchasing and inventory across all business units
  • EWM (Extended Warehouse Management) — serialized device tracking across U.S. sites
  • QM (Quality Management) — batch records, inspection lots, quality notifications
  • SD (Sales & Distribution) — order management across units with different customer bases
  • FI/CO (Finance & Controlling) — SOX-controlled financial reporting and cost accounting
  • MDG (Master Data Governance) — material, vendor, and customer master governance
  • SAP IBP (Integrated Business Planning) — demand and supply planning
  • SAP GRC Access Control — Segregation of Duties, reviewed by Big 4 auditors at each phase

That's a lot of moving parts. And every one of them had to connect to nine external systems simultaneously.

The Integration Reality

I want to be honest about this part because I don't think it gets talked about enough in SAP project write-ups.

The integration landscape for this program included:

External System Function
Model NRevenue management and pricing
SalesforceCRM and customer management
Oracle CPQConfigure, price, quote
ServiceMaxField service management
CoupaProcurement and supplier management
VertexTax calculation
SAP CommissionsSales commission management
LanveraDocument output and customer communications
Power BIReporting and executive dashboards

Nine integrations running through SAP Middleware and PI. Each one a data contract between two systems that don't naturally speak the same language.

Here's what I learned: integrations don't break because of technology. They break because of data. Unexpected data formats, missing fields, values that exist in one system but mean something completely different in another — these are what cause MW and PI failures. We had failures during SIT cycles that stopped testing in its tracks. In those moments the instinct is to look at the technical layer first. More often than not, the root cause was data.

What helped us move through those failures was thorough, structured testing — not just happy path scenarios but deliberately testing edge cases, bad data, and boundary conditions before go-live. Every time we skipped that discipline, we paid for it later.

If you're heading into a multi-integration SAP rollout: test the data, not just the interface.

The Worst Moments — and What They Taught Me

I'm not going to pretend this program ran smoothly. There were difficult periods, particularly during SIT cycles when multiple deliverables failed close together. Those moments are genuinely hard — the team is under pressure, stakeholders are watching, and the temptation is to find someone to blame or to rush fixes through without fully understanding the root cause.

What I found in those moments was that the answer was almost always the same: go back to the data.

A failed deliverable in SIT is rarely a configuration problem in isolation. It's usually a configuration built on assumptions about data that turned out to be wrong. When we took the time to trace failures back to their data root cause — rather than patching the symptom — we fixed things properly and didn't see them break again in the next test cycle.

That discipline is harder than it sounds when you're three weeks from a phase go-live and the pressure is real. But it's worth it.

The Lesson That Changed How I Work

Before this program, I thought about SAP implementations in terms of modules and workstreams. MM had its requirements. QM had its requirements. Integration had its requirements. Each track did its work and they came together at the end.

This project changed that completely.

I now think about SAP programs from an organizational perspective first — not a module perspective.

What does that mean practically? It means that when a business unit stakeholder describes a requirement, my first question is no longer "which module owns this?" It's "what does the data behind this requirement actually look like, and does it behave the same way across every system that touches it?"

Data speaks differently across systems. The same concept — a customer, a material, a serial number — can be represented in completely different ways in Salesforce, in legacy ERP, in Oracle CPQ, and in SAP. If you don't surface those differences during requirements gathering, they will surface during testing or cutover — at the worst possible time.

If I could go back to 2020 and tell myself one thing, it would be this: talk about data at the same time you talk about requirements. Every time. In every workshop. With every stakeholder.

Bring your data migration lead into requirements sessions. Ask business unit leaders not just what they need the system to do, but what their data actually looks like today. That conversation — uncomfortable as it sometimes is — will save you weeks of rework.

What MDG Taught Us About Governance

We implemented SAP MDG (Master Data Governance) as part of the template to manage material, vendor, and customer master data. It was valuable — but with an important caveat that I'd want anyone planning a similar program to understand.

MDG governs data going forward. It does not fix the data that already exists.

Each business unit we onboarded brought legacy master data shaped by years of independent operation. MDG's governance workflows controlled new and changed records beautifully — but the historical data still needed to be profiled, cleansed, and remediated before migration. That work had to happen alongside MDG, not instead of it.

The practical advice: activate MDG early — before business units start creating data in the new environment. If you wait until mid-program, you'll spend time cleaning up records that were created without governance in place. We learned that the hard way in early phases and adjusted for later ones.

SOX Compliance: Building It In, Not Bolting It On

Because we were building a fresh template, we had a genuine opportunity to design SOX controls correctly from the start. We used SAP GRC Access Control for Segregation of Duties across the full module landscape, with Big 4 auditors reviewing our configuration before each phase go-live.

The decisions that mattered most:

  • Design roles from scratch aligned to SoD rules — don't import legacy role conflicts into a new system
  • Configure system-enforced controls wherever possible — three-way match in MM, release strategies for purchase orders, approval workflows in FI/CO — rather than relying on manual compensating controls that auditors will question
  • Keep audit trails in SAP — not in spreadsheets, not in email chains, in the system

One thing the phased rollout surfaced repeatedly: each new business unit introduced role assignment scenarios the template hadn't anticipated. We built a GRC review checkpoint into every phase's go-live preparation. That checkpoint caught SoD conflicts before users went live rather than after — and it was worth every hour it took.

Looking Back: Was It Worth It?

Yes. Without hesitation.

What makes me say that isn't the go-live itself — it's what came after. I stayed with this client through multiple releases of this transformation. Watching the system stabilize, watching business units that had operated in silos start sharing data and collaborating through a single platform, watching the compliance posture strengthen phase by phase — that's genuinely satisfying work.

Medical device organizations exist to help patients. Better ERP systems mean better supply chain reliability, better traceability, better compliance — and ultimately better patient outcomes. That connection is real, even if it's several layers removed from the bedside.

To anyone on my team from that program who might read this: the work you did mattered. The frustrating SIT cycles, the late nights debugging integration failures, the data cleanup that nobody wanted to do — it all added up to something real.

What I'd Tell Someone Starting a Similar Program

A few honest pieces of advice from someone who has been through it:

1. Data and requirements are one conversation, not two.
Run them separately and you will regret it. Every requirement has a data reality behind it. Surface that reality early.

2. Integrations break because of data, not technology.
Invest in thorough integration testing with real, messy, edge-case data. Happy path testing will not prepare you for go-live.

3. Think organizationally, not by module.
Your stakeholders don't think in SAP modules. Neither should you when you're gathering requirements.

4. Activate MDG before people start creating data.
Governance after the fact is cleanup. Governance from the start is prevention.

5. SOX controls belong in the system.
If your control lives in a spreadsheet, it's a risk. Build it into SAP GRC and sleep better.

Final Thought

Managing this SAP S/4HANA rollout across multiple business units of a medical device company from 2020 to 2022 was one of the most challenging and rewarding programs of my career. The technical complexity was real. The data challenges were real. The pressure of SOX compliance across a phased rollout was real.

But so was the outcome.

If you're working through something similar — a multi-unit rollout, a complex integration landscape, a regulated industry environment — I'd genuinely enjoy discussing it in the comments. Every program teaches you something new. I'm still learning from this one.

entry.title: How SAP S/4HANA supports operational complexity in the Oil & Gas industry
entry.content:
Introduction

When I think of the most difficult fields to work with, the first one to come to my mind has to be, inevitably, Oil and Gas. I think everyone can agree with me when I say that Oil and Gas companies are extremely hard to operate, as they do so in environments where complexity is the standard: many different parts of the business depend on each other simultaneously. Daily operations depend on large networks of assets, field activities, supply chains, maintenance cycles, compliance requirements, and financial controls that all need to work together in real time.

From the outside, it is easy to assume that with these types of companies the biggest issue would be related to the size of the operations, since they tend to have thousands of employees, global operations and massive amounts of data. Nonetheless, the real challenge is making all departments, systems, and processes work together without difficulties. In other words, it is a problem of coordination, not of size.

Over the years, the system these companies manage starts changing little by little, which leads to different teams working differently instead of following one single and consistent process, and they also begin to work with varied versions of operational data. We are talking about an environment that, as time passes by, becomes harder and harder to manage.

This is where SAP S/4HANA becomes important, not because it eliminates complexity, but because it helps organizations operate through it with greater visibility and control.

When operational complexity starts creating friction

Oil and Gas companies are, in some aspects, the same as any other type of business. One of these similarities they share is that problems don’t suddenly appear one night and explode the following day. As the company evolves, small changes, which at the time may seem harmless, accumulate. Maybe a new software is added or a different way of working among employees is incorporated. Teams become more independent as they gain confidence and news acquisitions, with their respected technology and processes, are added. All these things that are necessary in the short-term cause work to become fragmented and an environment where even the most simple activities, like generating reports or tracking inventory, is hard to manage.

We have seen cases where different departments within the same company are technically using operational data, but they are not using it in a synchronized way. This is one of the main reasons the company becomes slower and less efficient. I will mention, as examples, the maintenance team, the procurement team, and the finance department. In these cases, everyone was looking at operations from different perspectives and with different update speeds. Although everyone had the information, they had it distributed across multiple systems and processes that did not communicate consistently with each other. As a result, operational planning became slower, manual intervention was required more often, and decision-making became slower as the data couldn’t be used efficiently.  Not to mention the fact that, in Oil and Gas companies, this is particularly critical because a minimal maintenance delay can affect production schedules and have financial and operational consequences simultaneously.

SAP S/4HANA helps reduce this fragmentation by acting as a shared operational environment for the whole company. Instead of every department using separate systems and isolated workflows, they all use the same operational framework. SAP integration allows areas that depend heavily on each other to exist inside one connected environment with information being consistently shared across the whole organization.

I came across a situation like this some years ago, when we worked with a global company that supports energy and field operations around the world. This company had a very large amount of operational purchasing activity, but many of the SAP processes behind it were way too manual compared to the size of the business. When the company contacted us, they had already went through problems during maintenance shutdowns because a lot of sites were sending urgent material requests at the same time through SAP.

To help with this, we automated part of the SAP purchasing and approval process so urgent requests could move faster and teams didn’t have to spend so much time manually checking and coordinating everything. This helped them process materials faster during maintenance periods and reduced a lot of repetitive admin work between operations and procurement teams.

And that is my point: SAP S/4HANA, at least in the Oil and Gas environment, is the easiest and safest way of connecting processes in real time. One of the biggest operational pressures in Oil and Gas environments comes from the fact that many activities cannot simply “wait until tomorrow.” Maintenance schedules, field operations, procurement cycles, and inventory availability are often directly connected to production continuity. Even the smallest delay can affect the whole system in these kinds of fields.

One thing is clear: operational efficiency is not only related to production itself. In fact, if production goes well it’s because there is something else functioning behind it, and that has to do with how quickly companies are able to react when conditions change. This is one of the reasons why SAP S/4HANA has become increasingly important in asset-intensive industries. It allows organizations to build more connected operational environments where information can move faster between teams and operational areas. In industries where downtime, delays, or lack of visibility can quickly translate into operational and financial consequences, having more standardized and integrated processes becomes less of a technological improvement and more of an operational necessity.

Conclusion

In Oil & Gas, circumstances almost never slow down enough to permit a full reset. Teams have to keep the wheels turning as production can’t stop, maintenance issues pop up, materials need to be in the right place, and decisions have to be made fast, all under constant stress. Minor flaws in how companies do things can snowball into much bigger problems if no one catches them early.

That’s where SAP S/4HANA really shows its value. Sure, it covers all the basics, but it goes further: it helps companies build smoother, more connected operations. Processes speed up, data gets more reliable, and teams don’t have to waste so much time tangled in paperwork or chasing down info. In a fast-paced world like Oil & Gas, being able to respond quickly and stay consistent isn’t just nice to have, it’s essential.

entry.title: Why ERP still defines the limits of AI in the enterprise
entry.content:
Introduction

There’s been a lot of noise around AI and how much it’s changing enterprise systems. It is undeniable that changes are here to stay. We’ve seen teams getting better forecasts, automating things that used to take forever, and catching issues earlier than before. Even we as an enterprise have evolved into something that I have no idea how it would function if AI suddenly disappeared one day.

But after a while, the conversation shifts. You can do countless of things with AI, and I think that is what everyone else is focusing on. And what else? What comes after checking whether AI works or not? What do you actually do once it's there? Getting recommendations is not the hard part anymore. You can have a model suggesting changes, flagging risks, or pointing at opportunities, and most of the time it makes sense. The problem shows up later, when you try to use that in real processes. That’s usually where things start to slow down, and where ERP suddenly matters again.

The Gap Between AI Insights and Execution

We’ve seen this happen more than once. Companies expect faster decisions because they have better insights, but once those decisions need to move through finance, operations, or logistics, everything gets more complicated. Systems don’t always adapt quickly, processes depend on each other, and what looked like a simple adjustment ends up taking way more effort than expected.

In many scenarios, the real challenge emerges when you have to close the execution gap, and not when you have to add more intelligence, as many people may think. It’s something that comes up all the time in conversations with our clients: the need is there, the intent is clear, but the underlying ERP landscape just isn’t ready to move at the same speed. Closing that gap between expectation and reality is where most of the real work is happening today.

The struggle that most organizations have has todo with what comes after they have produce insights. How do you execute a decision through a system that is so structured and interconnected? Is thanks to SAP S/4HANA —and similar ERP platforms— that decision has the opportunity to become reality.

On paper, the issue is simple. AI generates an insight, the business evaluates it, and then we, as organization, execute it. The thing is that, in reality, this chain encounters a vast amount of problems that lead to decisions getting delayed or partially implemented.

In one of the projects we worked on, this became very clear. The company, a global manufacturer in the energy space, was still relying on manual processes to count and track critical components. It wasn’t just slow, it also introduced errors and even safety concerns for the people involved.

By introducing AI-based image recognition, they were able to automate this process and significantly improve both productivity and safety. However, what became clear during the implementation is that the real value didn’t come from the AI model itself, but from how that information was integrated and executed across the operation. The data captured in the field needed to be synchronized with ERP systems, trigger updates in inventory, and align with warehouse and logistics processes in real time. Without that connection, the insight would have remained isolated. It was the ability to translate that intelligence into structured actions within the system that ultimately made the difference.

In another case, a client was using predictive models to anticipate demand changes and adjust procurement accordingly. The insights were accurate, but execution lagged behind. Translating those recommendations into actual purchase orders required multiple validations and manual interventions within the ERP system. By the time the adjustments were fully implemented, the conditions had already shifted. Once again, the limitation was not the quality of the insight, but the ability to execute it in time.

This is why the conversation around ERP is becoming relevant again, especially in the context of AI. It’s no longer just about having a system to run transactions, but about having a system that can absorb change and execute decisions without friction. Concepts like clean core, standardized processes, and flexible extensions are not just technical preferences anymore. They directly impact how fast and how effectively a company can act on what AI is telling them.

Conclusion

What we are seeing, then, is a shift in how these technologies relate to each other. AI is expanding what companies can see and understand about their business, giving them more options and better insights. ERP, on the other hand, continues to define what they can actually do with that information. The gap between those two layers is where many organizations are currently stuck.

The truth is that the companies that will get the most value out of AI are the ones that connect with the models to execution, and not necessarily the ones with the most advanced models. That means treating ERP not as a static backend system, but as a dynamic platform that enables change across the organization. AI might be redefining how companies think, but ERP still defines how they act. In the enterprise, action is where value is created.

entry.title: From Static Icon to Talking Tile: Adding a Dynamic Fiori Tile for Locked SAP Users
entry.content:
In my previous blog, I described how I built a mobile-friendly Fiori app for basic SAP user administration — lock user, unlock user, reset password, and change validity date.

The idea was simple: if you are on call, travelling, or supporting users across time zones, you do not always want to open SAP GUI from a laptop just to unlock one user. A small Fiori app on a mobile phone can be enough.

And then, of course, once the app was working, the next question appeared:

“Can the tile tell me something before I even open the app?”

At first, my Launchpad tile was static. It had a nice unlock icon, a title, and it opened the app. Technically correct. Visually acceptable. But operationally… it was silent.

I wanted the tile to say:

Unlock User
3 / 130
Locked users

That way, before opening the app, I already know whether there is anything to check.

This blog is about how I converted a static Fiori tile into a dynamic one using an OData endpoint.

 

 Figure 1. Shows Dynamic Tile used in Unlock User Fiori App

The small problem that started it

The application itself already had the logic to search SAP users, display locked users, unlock them, change validity dates, and so on.

But the tile did not know anything.

It was like having a restaurant sign that only says “Kitchen”, but never tells you if there are three orders waiting inside.

So the goal was:

Show number of locked SAP users / total SAP users directly on the Fiori Launchpad tile.

For example:

3 / 130
Locked users

And I also wanted some simple color logic:

Locked users Tile status

0Positive / green
1 - 5Critical / orange
6+Negative / red

High-level idea

A dynamic tile needs a backend service.

The tile calls an OData URL. The OData service returns a JSON response with values such as:

{
  "d": {
    "title": "Unlock User",
    "number": "3",
    "numberUnit": "/ 130",
    "info": "Locked users",
    "numberState": "Critical",
    "infoState": "Critical"
  }
}

SAP Fiori Launchpad then uses this response to display the dynamic number and status on the tile. SAP’s documentation describes dynamic app launcher tiles as tiles whose displayed information can be pulled from a backend system via OData, and the OData structure contains the properties used by the tile. (SAP Help Portal)

Step 1: Create a new OData entity for the tile

In my Gateway project, I added a new entity type.

Example:

Entity Type: TileInfo
Entity Set : TileInfoSet
Key        : id

The properties I used:

Property Type Purpose

idStringKey
titleStringTile title
subtitleStringOptional subtitle
iconStringSAP icon
infoStringFooter text
infoStateStringFooter status
numberDecimal/String-compatibleMain KPI number
numberDigitsInt32Decimal digits
numberFactorStringOptional factor
numberStateStringKPI status/color
numberUnitStringUnit text, in my case / 130
stateArrowStringOptional arrow indicator

After defining the entity, I generated the runtime objects in SEGW.

Step 2: Implement TILEINFOSET_GET_ENTITY

This is the ABAP method that feeds the tile.

The logic is intentionally simple:

  1. Count all users in USR02

  2. Count locked users where UFLAG > 0

  3. Return the locked number as number

  4. Return total users as numberUnit

  5. Set the color/status depending on thresholds

Here is the full method:

METHOD tileinfoset_get_entity.

  DATA lv_total  TYPE i.
  DATA lv_locked TYPE i.

  CLEAR er_entity.

  SELECT COUNT( * )
    FROM usr02
    INTO @lv_total.

  SELECT COUNT( * )
    FROM usr02
    WHERE uflag > 0
    INTO @lv_locked.

  er_entity-id           = 'UNLOCK'.
  er_entity-title        = 'Unlock User'.
  er_entity-subtitle     = ''.
  er_entity-icon         = 'sap-icon://unlocked'.
  er_entity-info         = 'Locked users'.
  er_entity-number       = lv_locked.
  er_entity-numberunit   = |/ { lv_total }|.
  er_entity-numberdigits = 0.
  er_entity-numberfactor = ''.
  er_entity-statearrow   = 'None'.

  IF lv_locked = 0.

    er_entity-numberstate = 'Positive'.
    er_entity-infostate   = 'Positive'.

  ELSEIF lv_locked <= 5.

    er_entity-numberstate = 'Critical'.
    er_entity-infostate   = 'Critical'.

  ELSE.

    er_entity-numberstate = 'Negative'.
    er_entity-infostate   = 'Negative'.

  ENDIF.

ENDMETHOD.

That is the whole “brain” behind the tile.

No magic. Just one small OData endpoint that answers one very useful question:

“How many users are locked right now?”

Step 3: Test the OData service

Before touching Launchpad configuration, I tested the service in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT.

Request:

/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST5_SRV/TileInfoSet('UNLOCK')?$format=json

Expected response:

{
  "d": {
    "id": "UNLOCK",
    "title": "Unlock User",
    "subtitle": "",
    "icon": "sap-icon://unlocked",
    "info": "Locked users",
    "infoState": "Critical",
    "number": "3",
    "numberDigits": 0,
    "numberFactor": "",
    "numberState": "Critical",
    "numberUnit": "/ 130",
    "stateArrow": "None"
  }
}

Once I got HTTP 200 OK, I knew the backend part was fine.

At this point, the tile still did not show anything dynamic. That is normal. The backend was ready, but Launchpad still had to be told where to read the data from.

Step 4: Configure the tile as Dynamic in Fiori Launchpad App Manager

In my case I used:

/UI2/FLPAM

I opened my technical catalog and selected the app descriptor item for my app.

Then, in the Tiles tab, I changed or verified the tile configuration:

Field Value

Tile TypeDynamic
TitleUnlock User
Tile InformationLocked users
Tile Iconsap-icon://unlocked
Refresh Interval300
Service URL/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST5_SRV/TileInfoSet('UNLOCK')?$format=json

The refresh interval defines how often the tile should refresh its dynamic data. SAP documentation for dynamic tiles also shows configuration through Launchpad tooling, including use of an OData service for real-time information display. (SAP Help Portal)

In some systems, if the URL with quotes causes problems, the encoded version can be used:

/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST5_SRV/TileInfoSet(%27UNLOCK%27)?$format=json

Step 5: Optional manifest configuration

If the app descriptor is maintained through the UI5 app manifest, the dynamic tile can also be described with an indicatorDataSource.

Example:

"indicatorDataSource": {
  "dataSource": "mainService",
  "path": "TileInfoSet('UNLOCK')",
  "refresh": 300
}

This tells the Launchpad which OData model and path to use for the tile indicator.

In my case, I still verified the final configuration in FLPAM, because sometimes the Launchpad catalog configuration is the place where the tile actually lives.

Step 6: Clear cache, because of course

This is SAP. The code can be perfect and still look wrong until the right cache is cleared.

The usual suspects:

/UI2/INVALIDATE_GLOBAL_CACHES
/UI2/INVALIDATE_CLIENT_CACHES
/IWFND/CACHE_CLEANUP
/IWBEP/CACHE_CLEANUP

Then I removed the old static tile from My Home and added the app again from the App Finder.

After a browser hard refresh, the tile finally started talking.

The result

Instead of a static tile with only an icon, I now had:

Unlock User

3 / 130
Locked users

And because numberState was returned as Critical, the tile displayed the number in orange.

Exactly what I wanted.

Not a dashboard. Not a full monitoring solution. Just a small piece of useful information directly where the user starts working.

Lessons learned

1. A dynamic tile is not a custom tile

This is an important distinction.

I did not need to build a custom SAPUI5 tile. A standard Dynamic App Launcher Tile was enough.

That means less frontend code, less maintenance, and less trouble.

2. The backend response format matters

The tile expects specific property names such as:

number
numberUnit
info
numberState
infoState

If the OData service works technically but the fields are wrong, the Launchpad will not display what you expect.

3. Always test in Gateway Client first

If the OData endpoint does not return the correct JSON in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT, there is no point debugging the Launchpad tile.

First make the service work. Then configure FLP.

4. Cache is part of the development process

I would love to say the tile changed immediately.

It did not.

Clear cache, remove the old tile, add it again, refresh the browser — and then judge the result.

5. Small UX improvements matter

This change does not unlock users faster by itself.

But it tells the support person whether there is anything to look at before opening the app.

That is the kind of small improvement that makes a custom Fiori app feel more useful.

Final thought

In the first version of my app, the tile was just a door.

Now it is a door with a small sign on it:

3 users are waiting.

And sometimes that is exactly what a support user needs.

Not another report.
Not another dashboard.
Just the right number, in the right place, before the first click.

entry.title: How SAPPHIRE 2026 directly influences your Cloud ERP Private modernization journey
entry.content:
Every SAP customer is on a different stage of its cloud modernization journey and those who haven’t yet started the discussions (and that could have happened due to a variety of reasons) must go through this blog.

At SAP Sapphire 2026, the conversation changed significantly.

SAP made two important announcements that together create a compelling—and urgent—case for customers running ECC On-Premise (ECC OP), S/4HANA On-Premise (S/4HANA OP), and ERP Private Cloud Edition (ERP PCE) systems to accelerate modernization toward Cloud ERP Private.

The message is becoming increasingly clear: customers who commit to modernization early will gain faster access to innovation, AI, and long-term supportability.

AI Innovation Is No Longer Reserved Only for Full Cloud Customers

One of the most impactful announcements at Sapphire 2026 was SAP’s decision to extend selected AI capabilities to ECC OP and S/4HANA OP customers.

Traditionally, many of SAP’s newest AI-driven innovations—especially those powered through Joule and cloud-native business processes—were positioned primarily for cloud ERP customers.

Now, SAP has opened a new path.

Customers running ECC or S/4HANA On-Premise systems who commit at least 50% of their on-premise maintenance base toward cloud adoption can gain access to selected AI scenarios, even while still operating their OP environments.

Based on the above, it is pertinent to share that cloud systems get all AI capabilities and on-premises systems will get a sub-set. It requires customers and SAP effort to make agents work on-premises, driving our cloud and on-premises differentiation. Note this applies to agents running on ERP systems, not migration agents.

To help accelerate the impact of AI to our customers’ businesses, SAP experts will help enable agentic AI for our new and existing Cloud ERP Private customers by activating up to 3 Joule Assistants in their ERP at no additional cost. Customers can select Joule Assistants that are available in their environments which will support common workflows such as finance, HR and supply chain.

Lastly, it is important to state that there is no change in SAP’s AI policy as AI agents require standardized systems to operate reliably, and the cloud is where SAP can guarantee this foundation, so the cloud remains our strategic focus. The activation of agents on on-premises systems requires services and engineering resources which we are offering to customers that commit to convert at least 50% of their current maintenance fee to the cloud. For those customers, we’ll provide strategic AI use cases so they can begin using and getting value from AI faster. Capabilities may vary based on technical requirements, level of customizations on existing systems, and may require underlying application as required by the agent. Please reach out to your SAP contact for more information.

Essentially, the SAP announcement acknowledges an important market reality:

  • Customers still need to derive innovation value during transition years.
  • AI adoption cannot wait for the “final phase” of ERP transformation.

However, there is an important nuance.

These AI capabilities will still come with boundaries and limitations compared to fully cloud-native environments. The deepest innovation velocity will continue to happen in Cloud ERP Private and public cloud solutions. Which means this announcement should not be interpreted as a reason to delay modernization indefinitely.

Instead, it should be seen as a bridge. A bridge that allows customers to start realizing AI value while they accelerate toward their longer-term cloud ERP architecture.

The ERP PCE Transition Option May Not Apply to Everyone

Another major development shaping customer strategy is the upcoming SAP ERP, private edition, transition option service expected around 2028.

This service is intended to help ERP PCE customers transition more smoothly toward Cloud ERP Private.

At first glance, this sounds reassuring for customers who believe they have more time but there is a critical detail that organizations should not ignore:

The transition option service is expected to include a 2 TB database size criterion.

That means not every ERP PCE customer may qualify and that changes the planning equation entirely.

Organizations that assume they can “wait until 2028” may discover later that:

  • Their system size doesn’t reach the system eligibility criteria
  • Their landscape complexity requires longer preparation cycles
  • Data reduction and archiving activities take years, not months
  • Skilled transformation resources become increasingly constrained as deadlines approach

In other words, the later customers begin, the fewer options they may ultimately have.

ECC and S/4HANA OP Customers Should Treat This as a Strategic Window

The Sapphire 2026 announcements create an interesting transition period.

SAP is effectively giving customers:

  1. Early AI access during the transition journey
  2. A modernization path toward Cloud ERP Private
  3. Incentives to begin cloud commitment now rather than later

Modernization Is No Longer Just About End of Maintenance

For many years, ERP modernization discussions revolved around maintenance deadlines.

That framing is now outdated.

The real differentiator moving forward is innovation velocity.

SAP’s Sapphire 2026 announcements signal a future where AI, automation, and cloud-delivered capabilities become central to enterprise competitiveness—even for customers still operating hybrid landscapes.

But hybrid access is unlikely to be the destination.

It is the transition phase.

And for ECC OP, S/4HANA OP, and ERP PCE customers alike, the smartest move may be to use this phase not as a reason to delay transformation, but as an opportunity to accelerate it thoughtfully and strategically.

entry.title: Why SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise Strategy Matters More Than You Think
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire 2026, it became clear that SAP is not just evolving ERP. It's rethinking how enterprises operate. The Autonomous Enterprise is more than a new label. It reflects a shift toward systems that can actively drive outcomes using agentic AI. It changes how enterprise landscapes are designed, governed, and run.

SAP's Evolution Towards Agentic AI 

From an architectural standpoint, SAP’s journey reflects a deliberate and well‑orchestrated evolution—from foundational systems to intelligence, and now toward autonomy:

  • R/3 era: Built the foundation with client-server architecture
  • ECC era: Expanded into a digital core, integrating end-to-end business processes
  • S/4HANA era: Transformed performance with in-memory computing (HANA)
  • Today: Moving to cloud operations powered by data and AI, unifying applications and insights
  • Next: Evolving into the Autonomous Enterprise, where agentic AI systems help run business processes

The foundation is already in place. In most organizations, ERP remains the most valuable source of data that AI and agents can use to drive better decisions.

However, moving from cloud ERP to a more agent-driven ERP is not a simple shift. It requires the right foundation across data, development, and governance. This is exactly where SAP is focusing.

From Complex Deployments To Simple Strategy 

If you’ve moved from ECC to S/4HANA, you’re on the right path. If you’ve taken the next step and moved S/4HANA to the cloud, you’re already ahead of many organizations. And if you’re layering in the tools and capabilities needed to bring AI into your ERP landscape, you’re not just keeping up—you’re positioning your enterprise for what’s next.

What SAP is doing now is bringing together capabilities that were previously spread across different areas:

  • BTP (Business Technology Platform): the foundation for apps, integration, and AI
  • Business Data Cloud (BDC): a unified data layer connecting SAP and non-SAP data
  • All combined into one: The SAP Business AI Platform 

This platform connects tools, applications, processes, and data into one cohesive environment. In simple terms, SAP is moving away from a collection of separate tools toward a single platform that can support both today’s needs and the future vision of autonomous operations.

SAP Business AI Platform introduces a simplified, strategic architecture built around three core layers—build, context, and governance—designed to make agentic AI scalable, reliable, and enterprise-ready. 

With the Business AI Platform, organizations now have everything they need to build agents. The next step is understanding how those agents actually come together to get work done in an agent-driven environment.

The Autonomous Execution 

From a user perspective, this is designed to feel simple and intuitive. A business user works through Joule Work using natural language, asking questions or triggering actions, while the complexity of agentic AI stays behind the scenes.

Users get clear insights, recommendations, and outcomes, without having to manage processes, systems, or integrations. The experience is focused on getting work done, while the orchestration and execution are handled invisibly in the background.

In the example above, the Sourcing Manager interacts with Joule Work at the top, which acts as the entry point. Behind that, a Sourcing Assistant coordinates multiple domain agents across finance, procurement, customer experience, and HR.

Each of these agents is tied to a specific Joule persona and executes predefined scenarios such as creating a purchase requisition or detecting a supply chain delay. These scenarios access enterprise data from different domains like finance, spend, supply chain, HR, and customer experience.

The agents collaborate, retrieve data, and execute workflows across systems, but all of that activity remains abstracted from the user, who only sees the final outcome and any relevant information needed to make decisions.

The Key Takeaways

  • SAP Autonomous Enterprise is the evolution from Cloud ERP to Agentic ERP
  • SAP Business AI Platform is the platform for building, contextualizing, and governing AI
  • SAP isn't just layering AI on ERP they are reimagining the future of how work is done
  • Organizations that have adopted SAP S/4HANA and Cloud are well positioned 

SAP Customer Advisory

If your organization is planning or evaluating an SAP strategy, our team at SAP Customer Advisory can help. We offer tailored guidance to align your architecture vision with business goals, accelerate transformation, and maximize value creation.

You can reach out to me directly to explore how SAP can help shape your architecture vision and drive successful outcomes — connect with me on LinkedIn.

entry.title: SAP Joule Document Grounding with Google Drive - Connecting AI to Enterprise Knowledge (Video Demo)
entry.content:
Enterprise AI becomes significantly more powerful when it can access organization-specific knowledge instead of relying only on generic model responses.

With document grounding in SAP Joule, Joule can retrieve information from enterprise repositories such as Google Drive or SharePoint and generate contextual, reliable responses using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

In this blog, I’ll demonstrate how to connect SAP Joule with Google Drive and enable document grounding using SAP BTP.


What is Document Grounding?

Document grounding allows Joule to retrieve enterprise documents before generating responses.

This helps:

  • Improve response accuracy
  • Reduce hallucinations
  • Provide organization-specific answers
  • Show source references for transparency

Typical grounded content includes:

  • SOPs
  • Functional specifications
  • HR policies
  • IT procedures
  • Internal process documentation

Demo Scenario

For this demo:

  • Google Drive is used as the document repository
  • SAP Joule is connected through the Document Grounding service
  • A RAG pipeline indexes enterprise documents
  • Joule retrieves information directly from grounded documents

I tested two Joule instances:

  • One without grounding
  • One with document grounding enabled

Steps to Enable Document Grounding in SAP Joule

Prerequisites

Ensure you have:

  • Access to SAP Joule
  • Document Grounding service entitlement
  • Google Cloud access
  • Google Drive repository containing documents

Step 1 — Subscribe to SAP Joule and Grounding Service

In your SAP BTP subaccount:

  1. Subscribe to:
    • SAP Joule
    • Document Grounding service

  2. Once the grounding service is available:
    • Create a service instance
    • Generate a service key

The service key will be used for authentication while creating and managing the RAG pipeline.

Step 2 — Create a Google Cloud Service User

In Google Cloud Platform:

  1. Create a Service Account
  2. Generate credentials for the service account
  3. Download the JSON credentials file

This service user will allow the grounding service to access Google Drive content securely.

Step 3 — Create a Google Drive Folder and Grant Access

  1. Create a dedicated folder in Google Drive

Example:
SAPJouleDataGrounding

  1. Upload the documents you want Joule to access:
    • PDFs
    • PPTs
    • Text files
    • Images

  2. Grant View access on the folder to the Google Cloud service user created earlier.

This allows the grounding pipeline to index and retrieve content from the repository.

Step 4 — Create and Verify the Grounding Pipeline

Using Postman or any API testing tool:

  1. Authenticate using the grounding service key
  2. Create a new grounding/RAG pipeline
  3. Configure:
    • Google Drive as the repository
    • Folder details
    • Service account credentials
  4. Activate the pipeline

Once created, verify the pipeline status.

In my demo, the pipeline successfully served content from the Google Drive folder, confirming:

  • Repository connectivity
  • Successful indexing

Step 5 — Test in SAP Joule

Now launch SAP Joule and ask organization-specific questions.

Example:

“What’s the process to raise SE38 FFID?”

or

“Tell me about Auralis custom process for vendor onboarding.”

With document grounding enabled, Joule retrieves the relevant enterprise documents and generates contextual responses along with source references

Demo Video

I have also included a dedicated demo video showcasing:

  • End-to-end setup of document grounding
  • Google Drive integration
  • RAG pipeline creation and verification
  • Grounded vs ungrounded Joule responses
  • Enterprise knowledge retrieval in action using SAP Joule

The video provides a complete walkthrough of the configuration process along with a live demonstration of grounded AI responses.

Thank you for reading this blog.

I will also be creating more content around custom SAP Joule Agents using Joule Studio and sharing additional demos, integrations, and enterprise AI use cases here.

Stay tuned for more SAP Joule and AI-related content.

entry.title: Service Bindings with OAuth 2.0 for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition
entry.content:
This post outlines the procedure to specify client credentials required for SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition systems to communicate with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition, using OAuth 2.0 client authentication. OAuth 2.0 provides a secure, token-based approach that is required for newer integration scenarios with SAP BTP services.

Prerequisites

Before starting the procedure, ensure the following requirements are met:

  • Initial setup in SAP BTP has been performed manually or via the Set Up an Account for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition booster
  • Required country/region-specific processes are active for your subaccount.
  • SAP Note 3540443 – Electronic Communication Integration Framework: Implementation Overview has been implemented.
  • You have the Space Developer or Org Manager role in your SAP BTP subaccount.

Note: If initial setup for a process or business system is already complete, it does not need to be repeated for additional processes in the same subaccount region.

Step 1: Create the Service Binding

    • Navigate to the Subaccount — In the SAP BTP cockpit, go to the subaccount where the service binding will be created.

       

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions: Choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance: Select the service instance created during the initial setup of the cloud edition.

 

    • Create Service Binding: In the Service Bindings section on the right, choose Create and fill in the fields as follows:

 

  • Finalize: Choose Create.

Special Instructions for France (E-Invoices and E-Reports)

If integrating to activate the E-Invoices and E-Reports (France) process, repeat the steps above but select 'Peppol Exchange' as the process instead of 'Other Processes' in the binding configuration.

 

Step 2: Retrieve the OAuth 2.0 Credentials

After creating the service binding, you need to retrieve the credentials to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system.

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions — In the SAP BTP cockpit, in the navigation area, choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance — Under Instances, choose the service instance of the cloud edition. The details screen for the service instance expands on the right side.

 

    • View Credentials — In the details screen, choose a service binding name to open the Credentials screen.

 

  • Retrieve Details — The Credentials screen displays the url, clientId, clientSecret, and drccApiUrl for this service binding.

     

Note: You need these credentials in the next steps when configuring the OAuth 2.0 client.

Summary
You have now successfully created a service binding using OAuth 2.0 client authentication in SAP BTP and retrieved the credentials needed for the integration. The next step is to use the clientId, clientSecret, url, and drccApiUrl to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system, which will enable secure communication with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

entry.title: Shape the Future of Purchase Orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud — Join Our Customer Engagement Initiative
entry.content:
Are you working with Purchase Orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition? We want to hear from you.

SAP is launching a Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) focused on the evolution of the Purchase Order experience — and we are inviting customers and partners to be part of the journey from day one.

What is a CEI?

A Customer Engagement Initiative is SAP's way of bringing customers and partners directly into the development process — not after decisions are made, but while they are still being shaped. As a participant, you get a seat at the table: early access to concept ideas, prototypes and solution approaches, with the opportunity to give structured feedback that directly influences the SAP product roadmap.

What's This CEI About?

Purchase Orders are at the heart of procurement — and we know that the way people work with them is evolving. This initiative will look at how customers actually use the Purchase Order applications today: the processes, the challenges and the opportunities for improvement.

A particular focus will be on AI-driven enhancements and intelligent automation — we will be sharing early-stage ideas and planned innovations in this space and want your honest input on whether they address real operational needs and deliver genuine business value.

This is your chance to make sure the next generation of SAP procurement capabilities reflects how you actually work.

Why Should You Join?

  • Influence the roadmap early — your feedback shapes what gets built, not just how it is polished
  • Get a preview of what is coming — including AI use cases and modernization concepts before they go public
  • Connect directly with SAP development teams — a direct line to SAP Product Management and Development
  • Be heard — the insights gathered through this CEI will serve as direct input for SAP's product direction in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition

How to Register

Registration is open now and closes on June 19, 2026.

Register here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/4229

If you are a customer or partner working with procurement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, we would love to have you involved.

entry.title: Leveraging AI for Predictive Data Quality in SAP S/4HANA Transformations
entry.content:
Leveraging AI for Predictive Data Quality in SAP S/4HANA Transformations

Introduction

One thing I’ve learned from running large S/4HANA programs is that data quality issues almost always show up later than anyone expects. Sometimes it’s during SIT, sometimes UAT, and occasionally right before cutover — the exact moment when nobody wants surprises. By then, even small inconsistencies can trigger downstream issues and create unnecessary pressure on both business and technical teams.

Traditional validation still matters, but it’s reactive. You only find the issue after the data has already moved through part of the migration process. That’s exactly where predictive analytics and AI‑driven techniques can make a real difference.

Key Takeaways

  • Traditional validation is necessary but often reactive in large S/4HANA programs.
  • AI helps uncover recurring patterns and subtle inconsistencies that manual checks usually miss.
